title: assessment2 report draft
body:
# FINS3666 Assessment 2 Group Project Draft

## Title Cross-Month Robustness of FX Strategy Parameter Templates with Funding and Microstructure Context

## 1. Topic Selection, Data Processing, and Implementation This project follows Option B from the assessment brief: detailed modelling of a key trading strategy input. Instead of building a new end-to-end strategy from raw tick data, we evaluate a pre-generated strategy-parameter grid and identify templates that remain robust across changing time regimes. The core hypothesis is that stable parameter templates can be identified through risk-adjusted performance consistency, not just maximum in-sample growth.

### 1.1 Data Scope and Rationale The primary dataset includes nine FX pair files: AUD_JPY, AUD_USD, EUR_AUD, EUR_JPY, GBP_JPY, GBP_USD, USD_CAD, USD_CHF, and USD_JPY. Each file is structured by monthly blocks (`month 0`, `month 1`, ...), and each block contains candidate templates with fields: order type, TP, SL, steps, wins, losses, loss streak, growth, drawdown, and risk.

This structure is appropriate for input robustness research because it explicitly captures performance variation under rolling monthly conditions. It also naturally supports out-of-time evaluation where model selection is made on earlier months and tested on later months.

Secondary datasets are: 1. `funding_raw.csv` (daily crypto funding rates for BTC, ETH, XRP, ADA, SOL; 2019-09-10 to 2024-12-31). 2. `SPY 20250115 Factset Trades.csv` (single-day trade tape with bid/ask context).

These secondary datasets are used as macro and execution context, not as direct causal features in the FX robustness model.

### 1.2 Cleaning and Standardization For FX files, the parser: 1. Validates schema. 2. Parses month markers into `month_index`. 3. Drops malformed or orphan rows. 4. Produces long format with deterministic sorting.

Derived fields include: `total_trades`, `win_rate`, `profit_factor_proxy`, `growth_log`, `score_safety_flag`, and `template_id = order_type|tp|sl|steps`.

The growth transform uses signed log scaling to reduce heavy-tail distortion while retaining direction: `growth_log = sign(growth) * log1p(abs(growth))`.

A safety indicator marks records where growth is positive and drawdown is below 0.60. This supports later ranking so that templates with extreme nominal growth but unacceptable drawdown are not over-favored.

### 1.3 Out-of-Time Design Per pair, months are ordered chronologically and split 70/30 into train/test. No leakage is allowed: all z-score references and winsorization thresholds are computed only on training rows.

Inside each pair, train-time robust score: `score = 0.40*z(growth_log) + 0.20*z(win_rate) + 0.15*z(profit_factor_proxy) - 0.15*z(drawdown) - 0.07*z(loss_streak) - 0.03*z(risk)`.

Template stability on train: `stability = median_score_train - 0.5*std_score_train + 0.2*safe_month_rate_train`.

Eligibility is coverage-based: `coverage_months_train >= max(4, floor(0.25 * n_train_months))`.

Top-3 templates are selected per pair from eligible candidates only.

## 2. Analysis and Evaluation

### 2.1 Main FX Robustness Findings Cross-pair recurring motifs suggest non-random persistence in a subset of template structures. The top reusable templates (by pair frequency and average OOT stability) were: sellLimit|2|1|1 (pairs=2, avg OOT stability=9.606); sellStop|2|1|4 (pairs=2, avg OOT stability=9.429); sellLimit|3|1|2 (pairs=1, avg OOT stability=10.998); sellStop|1|1|2 (pairs=1, avg OOT stability=9.235); buyStop|2|2|3 (pairs=1, avg OOT stability=9.113)

This does not imply universal alpha; it indicates that a small class of parameter families appears less sensitive to monthly regime shifts than alternatives in the same search grid.

### Pair AUD_JPY Top selected templates: #1:buyStop|2|2|3 (train stability -0.111), #2:sellLimit|2|1|1 (train stability -0.608). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log 9.5601, median drawdown 0.6018, worst drawdown 0.6523, safe-month rate 0.500. Baselines on OOT mean growth-log: A=2.8922, B=nan.

### Pair AUD_USD Train/test months: 25/11. Top selected templates: #1:sellLimit|7|1|3 (train stability 0.362), #2:sellLimit|2|2|1 (train stability 0.159), #3:sellLimit|6|2|2 (train stability 0.141). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log 8.1744, median drawdown 0.1111, worst drawdown 0.1111, safe-month rate 1.000. Baselines on OOT mean growth-log: A=8.9592, B=nan.

### Pair EUR_AUD Top selected templates: #1:sellLimit|2|1|1 (train stability -0.507). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log nan, median drawdown nan, worst drawdown nan, safe-month rate nan.

### Pair EUR_JPY Top selected templates: #1:sellLimit|3|1|2 (train stability -0.271), #2:sellStop|2|1|5 (train stability -0.363), #3:sellStop|2|1|4 (train stability -0.515). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log 10.4180, median drawdown 0.5681, worst drawdown 0.7965, safe-month rate 0.500.

### Pair GBP_JPY Top selected templates: #1:buyStop|1|1|1 (train stability 0.251), #2:buyStop|2|1|1 (train stability -0.353), #3:sellStop|2|1|4 (train stability -0.532). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log nan, median drawdown nan, worst drawdown nan, safe-month rate nan.

### Pair GBP_USD Train/test months: 25/11. Top selected templates: #1:sellStop|2|1|1 (train stability -0.686). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log nan, median drawdown nan, worst drawdown nan, safe-month rate nan.

### Pair USD_CAD Train/test months: 8/4. Top selected templates: . OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log nan, median drawdown nan, worst drawdown nan, safe-month rate nan.

### Pair USD_CHF Train/test months: 8/4. Top selected templates: . OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log nan, median drawdown nan, worst drawdown nan, safe-month rate nan. Baselines on OOT mean growth-log: A=-9.2085, B=nan.

### Pair USD_JPY Train/test months: 25/11. Top selected templates: #1:sellStop|1|1|2 (train stability 0.221), #2:sellStop|2|1|2 (train stability 0.153), #3:sellStop|1|1|4 (train stability -0.052). OOT selected-topK: mean growth-log 8.5904, median drawdown 0.2801, worst drawdown 0.4213, safe-month rate 1.000.

### 2.2 Baseline Benchmarking Two baselines were constructed for each pair: 1. Baseline A: highest train median growth-log. 2. Baseline B: lowest train median drawdown among train templates with positive median growth-log.

The selected top-k set is evaluated against both baselines out-of-time. This avoids a common pitfall where one criterion dominates (growth-only or drawdown-only) and creates unstable selection behavior.

### 2.3 Funding Regime Context For daily cross-asset funding mean, 25/75 quantile thresholds were: q25=0.00003374, q75=0.00013043. Regimes are defined as low/mid/high around these cutoffs. Funding standard deviation was 0.00028709, with tail observations: low-tail count=194, high-tail count=194.

Interpretation: elevated funding regimes typically proxy crowded long positioning and financing stress asymmetry. Even without direct FX mapping, these regimes are useful as macro risk overlays when deciding whether to trust high-growth templates in the next month.

### 2.4 SPY Microstructure Context Single-day SPY diagnostics show non-trivial execution frictions: median quoted spread=5.5302 bps, median effective spread proxy=4.8301 bps, return std=0.00021217.

This supports a practical lesson for FX template deployment: parameter robustness should be interpreted jointly with execution realism, not as a pure backtest ranking problem.

## 3. Critical Reflection

### 3.1 Strengths 1. Deterministic and auditable pipeline with explicit anti-leakage rules. 2. Robust scoring framework combining return quality and downside controls. 3. Out-of-time month-split design aligned with realistic model-selection workflow. 4. Pair-level and cross-pair synthesis, enabling both local and transferable insights.

### 3.2 Limitations 1. FX files are already result grids, not raw price series, so trade-level execution assumptions are inherited and not re-estimated. 2. Month indices are ordinal and may not correspond to identical macro windows across pairs. 3. Growth metric scale is highly skewed; log transformation helps but does not remove all comparability concerns. 4. Secondary datasets (funding/SPY) are contextual rather than directly integrated into FX template selection.

### 3.3 Top Three Recommendations for Real-World Improvement 1. Add regime-conditional selection: compute separate template rankings for low/mid/high macro states and switch template families dynamically. 2. Add execution-adjusted objective: include explicit slippage/cost penalties in monthly template scores to avoid over-selection of fragile high-growth candidates. 3. Move from univariate month ranking to probabilistic allocation: instead of winner-take-all top-k, allocate exposure by posterior confidence in stability and drawdown control.

These improvements require richer data (synchronized market states, liquidity proxies, and realistic cost models) and are expected to reduce out-of-time variance while preserving acceptable return efficiency.

## 4. AI Transparency Statement (Summary) AI assistance was used for workflow structuring, code drafting, and documentation synthesis. All transformations, formulas, and output checks were implemented and validated on local data with deterministic rules. Human review remained responsible for methodological choices, interpretation, and final deliverables.

## Appendix: Methodological Notes The robustness framework intentionally separates template scoring from template deployment. This separation avoids retrofitting deployment logic into the selection stage and makes validation decisions auditable.

A practical extension is nested walk-forward tuning where each month calibrates hyperparameters only on prior months and then applies frozen weights to the next month block. This would reduce optimism bias in parameter choice.

Another extension is hierarchical pooling by pair family. USD-quoted and JPY-quoted pairs can share partial priors while keeping pair-specific residual effects, improving stability for pairs with fewer month blocks.

Risk treatment can also move from fixed drawdown screens to CVaR-style penalties if monthly trade-sequence distributions are available. That change would make selection more sensitive to tail behavior rather than central moments alone.

From a governance angle, deterministic manifests and reproducible signatures should be versioned alongside submission artifacts so any post-hoc challenge can be replayed exactly. Team workflow can be improved by explicit ownership of data QA, modeling, validation, and reporting.

Additional technical note: future iterations can include bootstrap confidence intervals for out-of-time stability and sensitivity tests under alternative drawdown caps.
